sections.text: Welcome back to AP Biology. This is Module Six — Mendelian Genetics. We're inside College Board Unit Five, which is eight to eleven percent of the AP exam. Today's job: connect meiosis to inheritance, and figure out why offspring look like — and unlike — their parents.

Here's a number that should stop you. You share ninety-nine point nine percent of your DNA with every other human on Earth. The remaining zero point one percent — the part that makes you, you — gets shuffled in meiosis. Independent assortment alone gives roughly eight point four million unique chromosome combinations per gamete. Add crossing over and random fertilization, and the number of possible you's explodes. That's what we're unpacking today.

Three big moves today. First — meiosis: two divisions, one diploid cell becomes four haploid gametes. Second — Mendel's three laws, plus the ratios that come out of his crosses. Third — the non-Mendelian patterns that break the simple three-to-one rule. By the end, you'll be able to predict offspring ratios for almost any cross.

Big picture. Meiosis takes one diploid germ cell — that's two-N, forty-six chromosomes in humans — and produces four haploid gametes, each with N — twenty-three chromosomes. It does this in two divisions. Meiosis One separates homologous pairs. Meiosis Two separates sister chromatids. The result: four genetically unique haploid cells, ready to become sperm or eggs.

Two divisions, two jobs. Meiosis One is reductional — chromosome number is cut in half because homologous chromosomes pair up in Prophase One, cross over, line up in Metaphase One, and then separate. Meiosis Two is equational — sister chromatids separate, just like mitosis, but on haploid cells. Ploidy doesn't change in Meiosis Two. So: Meiosis One halves the number; Meiosis Two splits the duplicates.

Three sources of genetic variation in meiosis — memorize these. One: crossing over in Prophase One — non-sister chromatids of homologous pairs exchange DNA at chiasmata. Two: independent assortment in Metaphase One — each homologous pair lines up independently, giving two to the twenty-third — about eight point four million — combinations per human gamete. Three: random fertilization — any one of those gametes meets any of millions from the other parent. Multiply them together and the variation is staggering.

Mendel ran pea-plant crosses in the eighteen-sixties and gave us three laws. Segregation — the two alleles for a gene separate during gamete formation; each gamete gets only one. Independent assortment — alleles for different genes sort independently, as long as the genes are on different chromosomes. Dominance — in a heterozygote, the dominant allele's phenotype is the one you see; the recessive is masked but not erased.

The ratios you must know. A monohybrid cross — heterozygote times heterozygote, big-A little-a times big-A little-a — gives a three-to-one phenotype ratio and a one-to-two-to-one genotype ratio. A dihybrid cross — big-A little-a big-B little-b times the same — gives the famous nine-to-three-to-three-to-one phenotype ratio. But that nine-three-three-one only holds when the two genes are on different chromosomes. If they're linked, the ratio breaks.

Mendel got lucky — pea traits are clean. Real biology gets weirder. Incomplete dominance: red snapdragon crossed with white gives pink — the heterozygote is its own intermediate phenotype, so the ratio becomes one red, two pink, one white. Codominance is different — both alleles fully show. ABO blood: a person with the I-A and I-B alleles displays both A and B antigens on their red blood cells — that's type AB. Blended versus both-showing — don't confuse them.

Pause and try — two predictions. First: two pink snapdragons are crossed. What phenotype ratio do you expect in the offspring? Second: one parent has blood type AB; the other has type O. What blood types can their kids have, and in what ratio? Pause now. Work it out. Press play when you're ready.

Answers. Pink times pink is big-R little-r times big-R little-r — a monohybrid cross with incomplete dominance. Offspring come out one big-R big-R red, two big-R little-r pink, one little-r little-r white. That's the classic one-two-one. Second cross: blood type AB is I-A I-B; type O is little-i little-i. Every child inherits little-i from the O parent, and either I-A or I-B from the AB parent. So fifty percent are I-A little-i — type A — and fifty percent are I-B little-i — type B. No AB. No O. Ever.

Two more patterns. Multiple alleles — a gene has more than two versions in the population, even though any one person carries only two. ABO blood is the classic example: three alleles — I-A, I-B, and little-i — combining to make four blood types. Pleiotropy — one gene affects many traits. The sickle-cell allele is the textbook case: it changes hemoglobin, which causes anemia, pain crises, organ damage — and in heterozygotes, resistance to malaria. One mutation, a whole cascade.

Two more flavors. Sex-linked — most often X-linked recessive. Females are X X, so they need two recessive alleles to show the trait. Males are X Y — they're hemizygous, meaning only one X, so any recessive allele on it is always expressed. That's why red-green color blindness, hemophilia, and Duchenne muscular dystrophy hit males far more often. Polygenic inheritance — many genes contribute to one trait, so you get continuous distributions instead of discrete categories. Human height and skin color are polygenic.

The classic trap students fall into: incomplete dominance versus codominance. They sound similar — they're not. Incomplete dominance blends — pink from red and white, a brand-new intermediate phenotype. Codominance shows both at the same time, fully and separately — A antigens and B antigens both on the same cell, neither blended nor masked. The genotype-to-phenotype ratio is one-two-one for both, but the heterozygote looks completely different.

Recap. Meiosis One is reductional, Meiosis Two is equational. Three sources of variation: crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilization. Mendel's three laws: segregation, independent assortment, dominance. Three-to-one for monohybrid; nine-three-three-one for unlinked dihybrid. Non-Mendelian: incomplete dominance blends, codominance shows both, multiple alleles, pleiotropy, polygenic, sex-linked. And nondisjunction in meiosis causes aneuploidy — Trisomy Twenty-One is Down syndrome, X-X-Y is Klinefelter, X-O is Turner.

Your path forward. Read OpenStax Biology, Chapters Twelve and Thirteen — Mendel and patterns of inheritance. Drill Khan Academy AP Biology Unit Five — especially the Punnett square and probability problems. Do the assignment in your dashboard. And book an advisor check-in if dihybrid crosses or sex-linked pedigrees still trip you up — those are the two reliable test-day traps. Next time, we open the molecule itself: Module Seven — DNA Structure and Replication.
